title: AI Infrastructure Race Escalates as Export Controls Evolve
subhead: SoftBank's €75 billion French data center pledge and Meta's stock offering consideration highlight the accelerating AI infrastructure arms race, while the US revises semiconductor export policies and Taiwan investigates chip smuggling routes through Japan.

sections.heading: SoftBank Bets €75 Billion on French AI Infrastructure
sections.body:
SoftBank Group has committed up to €75 billion to develop 5 gigawatts of AI data center capacity in France, representing the largest AI infrastructure investment in Europe to date. The pledge, announced at the 2026 Choose France summit in Paris, leverages France's low-carbon nuclear power grid to support energy-intensive AI computing operations.

According to Reuters, the commitment forms the centerpiece of €93 billion ($108 billion) in total investment pledges secured by President Emmanuel Macron at the summit, with half destined for the SoftBank-backed data center project. The scale reflects how major capital allocators view AI compute infrastructure as the defining infrastructure buildout of the decade.

France's nuclear-powered grid offers a competitive advantage: data centers running AI training workloads require enormous electricity consumption, and low-carbon power addresses growing scrutiny of AI's environmental footprint. SoftBank's bet positions Europe as a secondary AI compute hub alongside the United States, though the investment remains dwarfed by US-based Stargate Project plans for $500 billion in domestic data center construction.
sections.whyItMatters: Europe gains significant AI compute capacity, but remains dependent on US and Asian chip manufacturers for the actual processors. The geography of AI infrastructure is settling into three major blocs: American, European, and Chinese.

sections.heading: Meta Weighs Major Stock Offering for AI Infrastructure
sections.body:
Meta is considering raising tens of billions of dollars through a stock offering to fund AI infrastructure ambitions, according to Financial Times reporting cited by Reuters. The move signals that even among the largest technology companies, the capital requirements for AI compute are straining existing financing channels.

Meta previously committed $65 billion to AI data center expansion in mid-2025, but competition for training compute has intensified. The company is racing against OpenAI, Google, Microsoft, and Amazon to secure GPU capacity and energy resources for next-generation model training.

The consideration of equity issuance—rather than debt financing—suggests Meta's management views AI infrastructure as equity-risk investment with uncertain returns, rather than predictable capital expenditure. It also reflects the competitive dynamics: companies that cannot match the investment pace risk falling behind in model capability.
sections.whyItMatters: The AI infrastructure race is shifting from debt-funded capex to equity raises, signaling higher risk perception and the scale of required investment. Capital markets become a constraint on AI development.

sections.heading: US Revises Semiconductor Export Policy for China
sections.body:
The US Department of Commerce's Bureau of Industry and Security issued a final rule effective January 15, 2026 that revises the export license review policy for advanced AI chips destined for China and Macau. The rule, implementing President Trump's December 2025 announcement, permits case-by-case review for Nvidia H200, AMD MI325X, and similar chips under specific security conditions.

According to the BIS press release, license applicants must demonstrate that exports will not reduce global semiconductor production capacity available to US customers, that Chinese purchasers have adopted export compliance procedures including customer screening, and that products undergo independent third-party testing in the United States to verify performance and security.

Under Secretary for Industry and Security Jeffrey Kessler stated the policy recognizes that export controls must evolve with technological change while protecting national security. The revision represents a shift from blanket denial to conditional approval, acknowledging that controlled sales can strengthen American technological ecosystems through revenue that funds research and development.

A CSIS analysis notes that export controls alone cannot substitute for comprehensive industrial and research policy measures to ensure US semiconductor leadership. China has responded with an all-out effort to stimulate domestic innovation, implementing a December 2025 policy requiring semiconductor manufacturers to use at least 50% domestically produced equipment.
sections.whyItMatters: The US is moving from pure technology denial to controlled engagement, attempting to maintain leverage while keeping American firms competitive. China's self-sufficiency drive has accelerated rather than slowed under sanctions.

sections.heading: Taiwan Investigates Chip Smuggling Route Through Japan
sections.body:
Taiwanese prosecutors are investigating three individuals suspected of smuggling Nvidia AI chips to China via Japan, according to Bloomberg. The case reveals ongoing circumvention of export controls through transshipment routes.

Prosecutors suspect at least one shipment of Nvidia AI chips was exported to Japan before being forwarded to China, exploiting trade relationships between Japan and Taiwan that face less scrutiny than direct Taiwan-China transfers. Taiwanese authorities seized 50 Super Micro servers with falsified documentation allegedly destined for Hong Kong.

The case highlights the difficulty of enforcing semiconductor export controls. Unlike chipmaking equipment—which is heavy, produced in small quantities, and technically challenging to transport—semiconductor chips are produced by the millions and easily concealed, as noted in CSIS analysis. Chinese enterprises have proven adept at smuggling proscribed chips through various forms of subterfuge.

Taiwan's semiconductor industry accounts for over 60% of global foundry revenue and more than 90% of leading-edge chip production, making it a critical chokepoint in enforcement. The investigation suggests smuggling networks are adapting to tighter controls by routing through third countries.
sections.whyItMatters: Export controls face enforcement limits when physical chips are small, valuable, and produced at scale. Transshipment through allied countries reveals gaps in the control regime that China continues to exploit.

sections.heading: Russia Launches Rassvet Satellite Constellation to Counter Starlink
sections.body:
Russia's Bureau 1440 launched the first 16 satellites of the Rassvet constellation on March 23, 2026, from the Plesetsk Cosmodrome, marking the beginning of a satellite internet network designed as a Russian alternative to Starlink. The project aims to deploy at least 300 satellites by 2030, providing broadband internet access with speeds up to 1 gigabit per second per user terminal.

According to WIRED reporting, the dual-use nature of Rassvet is evident from its military launch origin: the mission was carried out by the Russian Defense Ministry rather than Roscosmos. Russian President Vladimir Putin called the launch 'a great event,' while experts note that satellites 'capable of military functions' will find battlefield applications.

The project has received 100 billion rubles ($1.34 billion) from the Russian Ministry of Communications, with Bureau 1440 reportedly prepared to invest another 300 billion rubles. Rassvet terminals are described as several times larger and heavier than Starlink's, creating mobility limitations for military deployment.

The launch follows Starlink's demonstrated battlefield importance in Ukraine, where the system became 'one of, if not the most important components' of Ukrainian military connectivity, according to military analyst Franz-Stefan Gady. Russia had relied on thousands of contraband Starlink terminals smuggled through Central Asia before SpaceX blocked unauthorized access in February 2026.
sections.whyItMatters: Russia is building sovereign satellite communications infrastructure after Starlink dependency exposed vulnerability. The constellation will serve civilian and military needs, though technological gaps with Starlink remain significant.

sections.heading: Starlink Whitelist Disrupts Russian Battlefield Communications
sections.body:
SpaceX implemented a 'whitelist' system in February 2026 that blocked unauthorized Starlink terminals used by Russian forces in Ukraine, disrupting a key communications capability that Moscow had come to rely on. Ukrainian officials said the change followed discussions between Defence Minister Mykhailo Fedorov and Elon Musk.

According to BBC reporting, Ukrainian drone operators estimate Russian forces lost 50% of their offensive capacity in some areas after Starlink access was curtailed. The system had enabled Russian units to coordinate movements and guide drones with real-time video links in areas where military radios were unreliable or easily jammed.

Pro-war Russian military bloggers confirmed the impact. 'Elon Musk flipped the switch… our communications are in chaos,' wrote Yuriy Podolyaka, a Crimea-based blogger with nearly 3 million Telegram followers. Another channel, Dva Mayora, reported that Starlink deactivation had 'a negative impact on communications in our units.'

Russian forces are seeking workarounds: recruiting Ukrainian collaborators to register terminals, turning to fiber-optic lines and radio bridges, and attempting to use Russia's limited geostationary satellite systems operated by Gazprom Space Systems. The disruption has fueled anger in Moscow that Russian forces remain dependent on Western technology four years into the war.
sections.whyItMatters: Private companies now control critical military infrastructure. SpaceX's decision to restrict access demonstrates how commercial satellite operators can shape battlefield outcomes, creating precedent for future conflicts.

sections.heading: Nvidia Partners with Chinese Unitree for Humanoid Robotics Platform
sections.body:
Nvidia announced a partnership with Chinese humanoid robot maker Unitree for its first robotics system sold to researchers, combining Unitree's nearly 6-foot-tall H2 humanoid robot with Nvidia's Jetson Thor hardware featuring the advanced Blackwell GPU for on-device AI capabilities. The system includes Nvidia's Isaac GR00T humanoid-focused AI models and simulation systems.

According to CNBC, the announcement comes as Unitree seeks to raise 4.2 billion yuan ($620 million) through a listing on Shanghai's STAR board. The company disclosed that more than 40% of its revenue already comes from markets outside China, indicating global market penetration despite its Chinese origins.

At least four research institutions plan to use the H2 Plus humanoid: Seattle-based Ai2, ETH Zurich in Switzerland, Stanford Robotics Center, and UC San Diego's Advanced Robotics and Controls Laboratory. Notably, no China-based research institutions were listed among the initial partners.

Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang has predicted that 'physical AI' could become a market worth tens of trillions of dollars, with rapid growth expected in robotics over the next five years. The partnership with a Chinese company occurs amid ongoing US export controls on advanced chips, suggesting a nuanced approach to Chinese collaboration in robotics compared to semiconductor technology.
sections.whyItMatters: Nvidia's partnership with a Chinese robotics company demonstrates selective decoupling: collaboration continues in some technology domains while semiconductor exports face tight restrictions. Physical AI emerges as a strategic technology frontier.

bottomLine: Today's developments reveal an intensifying competition for AI infrastructure and technology sovereignty. SoftBank's €75 billion French data center investment and Meta's potential stock offering signal that AI compute capacity is becoming a national and corporate priority requiring sovereign-scale capital. US semiconductor export policy continues to evolve from blanket denial to conditional approval, acknowledging the limits of pure technology denial. Taiwan's investigation of chip smuggling through Japan demonstrates ongoing enforcement challenges, while Russia's Rassvet launch and Starlink's disruption of Russian communications show satellite internet becoming contested military infrastructure. Nvidia's partnership with Chinese Unitree in humanoid robotics suggests a more nuanced decoupling than pure technology denial—collaboration continues in some domains while export controls tighten on others.
sources: Reuters, Bloomberg, BBC, WIRED, CNBC, CSIS, Bureau of Industry and Security, Financial Times, The Guardian, Nikkei Asia
